questions.q: Lagrange interpolation constructs a polynomial through n data points. What is the degree of the resulting polynomial?
options: Exactly n - 1
At most n
Exactly 2n
Dependent on the data values
questions.explanation: Lagrange polynomials L_i(x) for i = 0, ..., n-1 are degree n-1. The interpolant P(x) = Σ f_i L_i(x) is a linear combination of degree n-1 polynomials, so it has degree at most n-1, and exactly n-1 if the leading coefficient is nonzero.

questions.q: Newton's divided differences method builds the interpolating polynomial incrementally. What is an advantage over Lagrange form?
options: Adding one more data point requires O(n) operations to update coefficients, not recomputing from scratch
Divided differences always produce smaller interpolation errors
Newton form avoids computing the Lagrange basis polynomials
It automatically selects the optimal node locations
questions.explanation: Newton form P(x) = a₀ + a₁(x - x₀) + a₂(x - x₀)(x - x₁) + ... has the property that adding x_{n} requires computing only one new divided difference coefficient a_n, O(n) work. Lagrange form requires recomputing all basis L_i, O(n²) work.

questions.q: The divided difference f[x₀, x₁] is defined as:
options: [f(x₁) - f(x₀)] / (x₁ - x₀)
f(x₁) - f(x₀)
[f(x₁) + f(x₀)] / 2
f(x₁) / f(x₀)
questions.explanation: The first divided difference is the slope of the secant line through (x₀, f(x₀)) and (x₁, f(x₁)). Higher-order divided differences f[x₀, ..., x_k] are recursively defined from lower-order ones and represent k-th order finite differences.

questions.q: Spline interpolation uses piecewise polynomial curves instead of a single high-degree polynomial. Why is this preferred?
options: Avoids Runge's phenomenon (oscillations at interval ends with high-degree polynomials) and maintains smoothness
Splines always interpolate with zero error
Splines require fewer data points than polynomial interpolation
Splines automatically choose optimal smoothness parameters
questions.explanation: Runge's phenomenon occurs when a single high-degree polynomial oscillates wildly near the boundaries for equispaced nodes, even if the underlying function is smooth. Splines (especially cubic with continuity conditions) avoid this by using low-degree pieces with smooth transitions.

questions.q: A natural cubic spline has what boundary conditions?
options: Second derivatives are zero at the endpoints: S''(a) = S''(b) = 0
First derivatives are zero at the endpoints
Function values match the derivative of the original function
The curvature is minimized everywhere
questions.explanation: Natural splines assume zero curvature (S'' = 0) at the boundaries. This reduces the system of equations from n+1 to n-1 unknowns for n+1 data points. Other choices (clamped, periodic) impose different boundary conditions.

questions.q: The Chebyshev nodes x_i = cos((2i - 1)π / (2n)) for i = 1, ..., n are optimal for polynomial interpolation. What problem do they solve?
options: They minimize the maximum interpolation error by clustering near the interval boundaries
They reduce the number of evaluations needed
They guarantee the interpolant is linear
They make the divided differences all equal
questions.explanation: Equispaced nodes lead to Runge's phenomenon. Chebyshev nodes cluster near ±1 and are sparser in the middle, controlling the growth of the product ∏(x - x_i). This minimizes the Lebesgue constant Λ and interpolation error.

questions.q: For a cubic spline with n data points, how many cubic polynomial pieces are needed?
options: n - 1 pieces, each connecting consecutive data points
n pieces with an extra boundary segment
n/2 pieces covering two points each
Just one piece covering all points
questions.explanation: With n data points (x₀, ..., x_{n-1}), there are n - 1 intervals: [x₀, x₁], [x₁, x₂], ..., [x_{n-2}, x_{n-1}]. Each interval has its own cubic polynomial S_i(x) defined on [x_i, x_{i+1}], with continuity of S, S', S'' at interior knots.

questions.q: Hermite interpolation differs from standard Lagrange interpolation by also enforcing that:
options: Derivative values (and possibly higher derivatives) match the original function at the interpolation points
The interpolant is a straight line between data points
Second derivatives are continuous everywhere
The polynomial has minimal degree among all approximations
questions.explanation: Hermite interpolation specifies both function values f(x_i) and derivative values f'(x_i) at each node. For n nodes with derivatives, the interpolant is degree 2n - 1. Cubic Hermite splines (locally) maintain C¹ smoothness using these conditions.

questions.q: The Lebesgue constant Λ_n bounds interpolation error as |f(x) - P(x)| ≤ (1 + Λ_n) dist(f, Π_{n-1}). Why is small Λ_n desirable?
options: Small Λ_n means the rounding error amplification is limited, ensuring numerical stability
Small Λ_n guarantees the interpolant is convex
Small Λ_n allows using fewer data points
Small Λ_n makes the interpolant unique
questions.explanation: The Lebesgue constant measures how much rounding errors in function values are amplified. For equispaced nodes, Λ_n grows exponentially (Λ_n ≈ 2^n / n), leading to instability. Chebyshev nodes have Λ_n ≈ O(log n), much better for high-degree interpolation.

questions.q: B-splines (basis splines) are used in modern computational geometry. Compared to explicit spline polynomials, B-splines offer:
options: Local control: changing one control point affects only nearby interpolant regions, not the entire curve
Faster computation through matrix multiplication
Exact interpolation of all data points
Automatic smoothness at all boundaries
questions.explanation: B-splines are basis functions with compact support: each B_i(x) is nonzero only on a small interval. Modifying a control point affects only splines with nonzero support in that region, giving locality. This is essential in CAD/graphics. Standard splines change globally with one data point adjustment.
